title: Lesson 370
transcript: There's one more threat modeling methodology that we need to talk about that you may encounter on the exam, and it's known as DREAD. DREAD's quite a unique threat modeling methodology compared to the other two that we've talked about, STRIDE and PASTA, in that DREAD is not actually about identifying threats, Rather, the whole point of dread is to prioritize threats that have been identified. And so, remember when we talked about stride, stride is kind of this quick and easy way of identifying threats, but it doesn't help you prioritize the threats in any way like pasta does. And so stride and dread are often used together, with stride being used to identify the threats and dread being used to prioritize the threats. So let's talk through this dread threat modeling methodology. And essentially the whole purpose of this, like I said, is to help you prioritize threats. How do you do that? Well, you need to evaluate, essentially, how easy is the threat to exploit? How damaging is it going to be to the organization? How easy is it to discover? By sort of thinking through all these things, you could think about, therefore, how concerning is a particular threat. And that's exactly what Dread does. And it's another one of these acronyms where the letters D-R-E-A-D each stand for a different key point that you're going to evaluate and then score to figure out how concerning a threat is. So the first thing you're looking at is damage. What's the total amount of damage? In other words, if this threat were to occur, how big of an impact would it have on the organizations, users, and so forth? And you give this a score between one and three. One meaning, yeah, not that big a deal. And three, a very significant amount of damage to the organization. So that's the first key point you would measure. The next one is reproducibility. And this is basically measuring how easily can the threat be reproduced. And this is actually an important measure because some threats are really easy to for an attacker to replicate. It works every single time. Maybe there's not even that many steps involved. It has really high reproducibility. And obviously, a threat that is easier to reproduce is more concerning. So again, you give it a score. The next thing you're looking at is exploitability. And this is very specifically measuring how much effort is involved by the attacker. Some threats, you just run a tiny bit of automated code and boom, you can exploit the threat. Other threats, this is one I'm thinking about related to S-MIME. It took like two weeks to exploit the threat. And the attacker had to send very specific messages at very specific times over a two-week period. And it didn't even work every time to reproduce a particular threat and to exploit a particular threat. So again, you're measuring exploitability, giving it a score. Next thing you're looking at, the A stands for affected users, and this is where you're looking at how many of your customers, how many of your own internal employees, how many people would be affected if this threat were to occur, and you give it a score. And then the last one that you're looking at is discoverability. And this is exactly what it sounds like. How easily can this threat be discovered? Maybe it's just a really simple scan that anyone could do of the internet and they can find this. Or maybe it's really difficult, really involved. Again, you give it a score. The easier it would be to discover the threat, the higher the score. And then you add these up. And so you go through this process for each threat. You come up with a total score. And if you're evaluating, say, 20 different threats, you're going to end up with different scores for each one. And that's how you can rank which threat is of greatest concern on down to least concern. So there you go, DREAD. One more threat modeling methodology you need to know about.
